coredata.dc:title: Enhancement of the Collective Optical Properties of Plasmonic Hybrid Carbon Dots via Localized Surface Plasmon
coredata.dc:creator: Emam, A.N.
Mostafa, A.A.
Mohamed, M.B.
Gadallah, A.-S.
El-Kemary, M.
coredata.dc:description: We report on the photophysical properties of the synthesized Plasmonic/Carbon dots (C-Dots/Plasmonic) nanohybrids prepared by the physical mixing of carbon dots with plasmonic nanoparticles such as gold and silver nanoparticles. Remarkable enhancements on the optical parameters such as absorptivity and fluorescence quantum yield, accompanied with the reduction in the rate of electron-hole recombination were observed for the hybrid nanostructure compared to pure carbon dots. This enhancement is due to enhancing the incident excitation field via localized surface Plasmon in the metallic part, which leads to increasing the exciton radiative recombination rate in the carbon dots, which is dependent on the spectral overlap in the absorption spectra. This plasmonic enhancement was more pronounced in the case of carbon dots – silver (C-Dots/Ag) nanohybrids than that of carbon dots – gold (C-Dots/Au) nanohybrids, due to low intrinsic loss and the degree of the overlap between the absorption spectra of silver nanoparticle and carbon dots. Furthermore, picosecond decay measurements show a decreased lifetime of carbon dots on the presence of plasmonic, due to the increased rates of radiative decay. The unique characteristics of the proposed Plasmonic/C-Dots can be used for application in single and multiple biological sensing or imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots
Plasmonic enhancement
Photoluminescence
Gold
Silver
Nanoparticles
Localized surface plasmon

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of quenching or enhancement efficiency
The proposed mechanism to describe the influence of plasmonic on the PLE properties of C-Dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrographs of (a) C-Dots/Au, (b) C-Dots/Ag, (c) C-Dots/PEI/Au and (d) C-Dots@PEI@Ag hybrid nanocomposites.
SAED patterns for (a) C-Dots/Au, and (b) C-Dots/Ag hybrid nanocomposites.

(a) Absorption/emission spectra of N-doped C-Dots and the absorption spectra of Ag and Au NPs, respectively. (b) the influence of plasmonic gold and silver nanostructures on the absorptivity for C-Dots.
Influence of plasmonic (a) gold and (b) silver nanostructures on emission spectra of N-Doped C-Dots in presence and absence of dielectric polymeric spacer (PEI).
Influence of the thickness (r) of spacer (PEI) on the plasmonic ehancement of N-Doped C-Dots in case of (a) gold and (b) silver nanostructures, respectively.

The influence of the content of plasmonic AuNPs on the Steady-State and CW Laser-excitation (λ = 405 nm) photoluminescence properties (i.e. FL-intensity, FL-QY for C-Dots/Au nanohybrids either in presence (a, b and c) of absence (d, e and f) of polymeric PEI spacer.

The influence of the content of plasmonic AgNPs on the Steady-State and CW Laser-excitation (λ = 405 nm) photoluminescence properties (i.e. FL-intensity, FL-QY for C-Dots/Ag nanohybrids either in presence (a, b and c) of absence (d, e and f) of polymeric PEI spacer.
Effect of plasmonic nanostructures (i.e. Ag & Au) on the radiative decay of the C-Dots in presence (a), and absence (b) of dielectric polymeric spacer upon picosecond excitation at 405 nm.
Schematic diagrams show the proposed mechanism for the quenching (a and c) and the enhancement (b and d) of N-Doped C-Dots fluorosence.
Comparison Study for the effect of dielectric polymeric spacer on the photoluminescence properties of Plasmonic/C-Dots nanohybrids.
Effect of plasmonic on the photoluminescence lifetime of N-doped carbon dots in presence of polymeric Spacer.
Effect of plasmonic on the photoluminescence lifetime of N-doped carbon dots when the carbon dots are in direct contact with the metallic particles.

head.ce:title: Enhancement of the Collective Optical Properties of Plasmonic Hybrid Carbon Dots via Localized Surface Plasmon

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Refractories, Ceramics & Building Materials Department, National Research Centre, Cairo, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Refractories, Ceramics & Building Materials Department, National Research Centre

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomedicine and Tissue Eng. Lab., Medical Center of Excellence, National Research Centre, Cairo, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanomedicine and Tissue Eng. Lab., Medical Center of Excellence, National Research Centre

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institute of Laser Enhanced Sciences (NILES), Cairo University, Cairo, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute of Laser Enhanced Sciences (NILES), Cairo University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Egyptian Nanotechnology Center (EGNC), Cairo University, Zayed City, Giza, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Egyptian Nanotechnology Center (EGNC), Cairo University, Zayed City

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of NanoScience and Nanotechnology, Kafr El-Sheikh University, 33516 Kafr El-Sheikh, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of NanoScience and Nanotechnology, Kafr El-Sheikh University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Refractories, Ceramics & Building Materials Department, National Research Centre, Cairo, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Refractories, Ceramics & Building Materials Department, National Research Centre

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report on the photophysical properties of the synthesized Plasmonic/Carbon dots (C-Dots/Plasmonic) nanohybrids prepared by the physical mixing of carbon dots with plasmonic nanoparticles such as gold and silver nanoparticles. Remarkable enhancements on the optical parameters such as absorptivity and fluorescence quantum yield, accompanied with the reduction in the rate of electron-hole recombination were observed for the hybrid nanostructure compared to pure carbon dots. This enhancement is due to enhancing the incident excitation field via localized surface Plasmon in the metallic part, which leads to increasing the exciton radiative recombination rate in the carbon dots, which is dependent on the spectral overlap in the absorption spectra. This plasmonic enhancement was more pronounced in the case of carbon dots – silver (C-Dots/Ag) nanohybrids than that of carbon dots – gold (C-Dots/Au) nanohybrids, due to low intrinsic loss and the degree of the overlap between the absorption spectra of silver nanoparticle and carbon dots. Furthermore, picosecond decay measurements show a decreased lifetime of carbon dots on the presence of plasmonic, due to the increased rates of radiative decay. The unique characteristics of the proposed Plasmonic/C-Dots can be used for application in single and multiple biological sensing or imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon dots (C-Dots) emerged as a new member of fluorescent nanomaterials family that was widely utilized in numerous detection methods of heavy metals, optical sensing, and bioimaging applications [1–9]. In addition, C-Dots have inspired extensive research interest owing to their excellent photostability, fascinating optical properties [10–12], and excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence. Their ability to be functionalized with different molecules according to their desired application [13–17], high stability and chemical inertness over time, among others [10–12,18]. Furthermore, their biocompatibility [11], nontoxicity [2] and water solubility [12]. Therefore, C-Dots have emerged as potential candidates of luminescent materials alternative to conventionally used organic dyes or inorganic semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) [19,20]. In addition C-Dots have widely been applied in many other applications such as photocatalysis [21], light-emitting diode (LED) [22], and energy conversion/storage [23].

Loading plasmonic nanoparticles (such as gold and silver) onto the fluorescent surfaces of carbon dots has many advantages as light-harvesting scope in visible light for the generation of more carriers ( e
-
(LUMO)
– h
+
(HOMO)
pairs) [24], due to the influence of the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [25]. This property is attributed to the collective oscillation of surface electrons of noble metals [26–28]. Therefore, the combination between the luminescence property and the plasmonic activity within a single nanoparticle would enable to form a class of multi-modal nanoparticles that could simultaneously utilize into both of theranostics or bio-sensing [29–33]. However, the challenge is how to combine luminescent fluorophores such as C-Dots with plasmonic nanostructures and enable the composite material to maintain the unique plasmonic and luminescent properties of both nano-components. The luminescent property of C-Dots can be affected by metal nanoparticles, which can be either enhanced or quenched due to the competition between field enhancement and non-radiative damping arising from luminescence resonance energy transfer to surface plasmon [34,35].

However, few studies have been devoted to tracking the influence of plasmonic nanostructures on the photoluminescence (PLE) of C-Dots [36–45]. In these studies, the fluorescence quenching mechanism was observed upon the direct attachment of C-Dots to plasmonic nanostructures due to the carrier transfer from the luminescent fluorophore to the Fermi level of a metal part [36–39,46]. In contrast, a remarkable fluorescence enhancement was monitored upon the influence of plasmonic NPs on the PLE properties of C-Dots in the presence of spacer in-between, which leads to the presence of other effect known as "Metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF)" [39]. In such mechanism, the significant radiative emission attributed to a strong interaction or overlap of the local electromagnetic field of excitons in the emissive layer in C-Dots, and surface plasmons [39–45]. In another pathway, the enhanced plasmonic fluorescence of C-Dots has been investigated through the sandwich or core-shell nanostructured system [42–45]. Besides the C-Dots based-luminescent fluorophores, such system formed of metal nanostructures can act as a metal core or shell [42–45]. In addition, a dielectric spacing layer such as silica [39,42–44], polymer [46] or polyelectrolyte [13,14]. It has been well established that the interactions between fluorophores and metal nanoparticles result in an increased photostability [32,46], fluorescence enhancement [47], and a decreased lifetime due to the increased rates of system radiative decay [48].

However, less attention has been paid to gain information about the mechanism explaining the influence of plasmonic on PLE properties of carbon dots using Time-resolved fluorescence measurements. In this contribution, we report the effect of plasmonic on the photophysical properties of C-Dots in Plasmonic/C-Dots nanohybrid systems in the presence or absence of dielectric polymeric spacer such as Polyethyleneimine (PEI) using steady-state and picosecond time resolved emission studies.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA, C6H8O7·H2O, 99.0%) and Tri-sodium citrate dihydrate (HOC(COONa)(CH2COONa)2·2H2O) were purchased from LOBA Chemie. Ethylenediamine (EDA, NH2CH2CH2NH2, 99.0%) was pursuched from Alpha Chemke. Sodium Borohydride (NaBH4, 85%) was pursuched from WinLab Ltd. Silver Nitrate (AgNO3, 99%), Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) (HAuCl4·3H2O, 99.99%) and Polyethyleneimine (PEI, 50 wt% solution in Water, Mn. 1200, Mw. 1300) were pursuched from Sigma Aldrich.

Plasmonic/C-Dots hybrid nanocomposites have been synthesized via physical mixing of different stoichiometric ratio of plasmonic nanostructures (i.e., Ag or Au NPs) with C-Dots prepared (Plasmonic NPs: C-Dots, 1:1–1:20 v/v), as previously reported [46]. An appropriate concentration about 0.2 mg/ml of N-Doped C-Dots has was used in the fabrication of the proposed nanohybrids. In addition, the molar concentration for the plasmonic nanostructures (i.e. Au or Ag NPs) was calculated based on the final content for plasmonic ions into the final solution for the obtained nanohybrids.

Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, JOEL – JEM 2100), was used to investigate the micrograph of obtained samples under operating voltage 200 kV. Furthermore, the crystallographic structure has been investigated via the selected area electron diffraction (SAED).

Steady-state absorption and emission spectra were recorded on T80 (PG Instruments) and Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrophotometers, respectively. The Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of C-Dots and Plasmonic/C-Dots nanohybrids were measured using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution (QY = 54%) as a reference standard [49]. The absorbance of the standard and samples at the excitation wavelengths and the fluorescence spectra of the same solutions were measured at a same optical density. The quantum yield of samples has been calculated according to the following Eq. (1):
(1)
Q
Y
C D o t s
= Q
Y
D y e
×
(
I
C D o t s
I
D y e
)
×
(
η
D y e
2
η
C D o t s
2
)
Where QYC-Dots is the quantum yield of the C-Dots, and QYDye is the quantum yield of dye. IC-Dots is the integrated emission band of the C-Dots and IDye the integrated emission band of dye. ηC-Dots is the refractive index of the C-Dots and (ηDye) the refractive index of dye at the excitation wavelength [49].

Meanwhile, the spectral profiles for both C-Dots and Plasmonic/C-Dots hybrid nanocomposites were performed at different excitation wavelength. Typically, a continuous wave (CW) diode laser at 405 nm (Laser wave LWVL 405 100555) has been used to excite the samples, and the emission was collected and focused to a spectrograph (Stellernet Inc. EPP 2000) using an optical fiber.

Emission decays were measured by Picosecond time-correlated single photon counting system (FluoTime 200, PicoQuant) described before [50,51]. The sample was excited using 40 ps pulsed laser λ = 405 nm with repetition rate 20 MHz. The emission signal was collected at the magic angle. The instrumental response function (IRF) of the apparatus was typically 65 ps. A multi-exponential function convoluted with the IRF signal was fitted to the emission decays using the Fluofit 4.4 package (Picoquant). The quality of the fits was characterized in terms of residual distribution and reduced χ2 values. All optical measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions. A third order exponential fit for all of the variations of samples under study is used, as in the following equation:
(2)
I ( t ) =
A
1
exp
(
− t
/
τ
1
)
+
A
2
exp
(
− t
/
τ
2
)
+
A
3
exp
(
− t
/
τ
3
)
Where τ
1
, τ
2, and τ
3 are decay constants of the fit function, while A
1
, A
2, and A
3 are the pre-exponential factors. The number of components in the fitness function is based on less variation residual, and on the smallest value for χ2 (The square of the differences between the fit and the experimental points). All fit values that presented in this work have 10% errors fit as a maximum error.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of quenching or enhancement efficiency
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorosence quenching and enhancement efficiencies in the presence of plasmonic nanostructures such as gold and silver nanostructures have been investigated using Stern-Volmer equation
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
S
*
[ Q ]
, which correlates the integrated fluorosence F, at different concentration of plasmonic nanostructures [Q]. In this case, the plot is linear due to the existance of static quenching mechanism. While, in some cases the plot of (F
o
/F) vs. [Q] shows a degree of non-linearity, which indicates the existance of both static and dynamic quenching mechanism. In this case, the modified Stern-Volmer plot should be used, when the static and dynamic quenching/enhancement mechanisms occurs simultaneously as quadratic polynomial fitting as follows;
F
0
/ F = 1 +
(
K
D
+
K
S
)
*
[ Q ]
+
(
K
D
K
S
)
[ Q ]
2
.

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements of C-Dots were measured by applying a computer-controlled VA-Standard 663 and μAutolab Type III potentiostat of Metrohm AG Suisse. The measurements were performed at a constant scan rate of 100 mV/s, with scanning range ~ − 2.5–2.5 V. Ag/AgCl wire and a Pt coil were used as the reference and counter electrode, respectively. The electrolytes solutions containing an aqueous solution of 1.0 mM of C-Dots and 0.1 M sodium nitrate as a supporting electrolyte. The electrolyte solutions were treated with ultra-high purity N2 for 10 min before the acquisition of electrochemical data, and the electrolyte solution was blanketed with a nitrogen atmosphere during the entire experimental procedure [52,53].

The morphological structure of the as-prepared Plasmonic/C-Dots nanohybrids has been investigated using TEM and SAED techniques as shown in
Figs. 1 and 2, respectively. TEM/HR-TEM analysis revealed the spherical morphology of C-Dots, with an average size of 5.5 ± 1.5 nm. Whereas, the observed average particle sizes for Au and Ag NPs were ≈ 14.5 ± 1.5 and 13 ± 1.5 nm, respectively. In addition, the presence of low-contrast C-Dots around the surface of high-contrast plasmonic nanostructures (i.e., Au and Ag NPs) signifying the successful formation of Plasmonic/C-Dots nanohybrids, either through the direct adsorption to metallic surface (Fig. 1a & b) or spaced in the presence of dielectric polymeric spacer such as PEI that form Plasmonic/PEI/C-Dots nanohybrids, Fig. 1(c and d). In Plasmonic/PEI/C-Dots nanohybrids, the average thickness of the spacer (r) was found to be 6.32 and 3.35 nm for C-Dots/PEI/Au and C-Dots/PEI/Ag nanohybrids, respectively (See Fig. 1c & d).

As can be seen from Fig. 2a, the crystallographic structure of C-Dots/Au nanohybrids exhibits two characteristic diffraction patterns at the interplanar distance (d
hkl ), recorded at 4.42 and 1.66 Å, that can be assigned to the (002) and (004) crystallographic planes of Graphite, respectively (ICCD 01-075-1621), suggesting the formation of C-Dots [54]. Another, two diffraction patterns were recorded at the d
hkl of 2.43 and 1.475 Å, corresponding to the (002) and (004) crystallographic planes of gold (ICCD 00-004-0784). Furthermore, a SAED patterns at d
hkl of 1.206 Å was obtained due to the combination of (110) and (311) crystallographic planes of graphitic C-Dots and gold, respectively, Fig. 2a. Moreover, C-Dots/Ag hybrid nanostructure exhibits four diffraction fringes at d
hkl of 3.31 and 1.67 Å corresponding to the (002) and (004) reflection plans of graphitic C-Dots. In addition, the diffraction patterns at 1.478 and 1.0896 Å are corresponding to (220) and (222) reflection plans of silver (ICCD 00-004-0783), as shown in Fig. 2b.

Fig. 3 shows the absorption and emission spectra of C-Dots, Plasmonic/C-Dots, and Plasmonic/PEI/C-Dots nanohybrids, respectively. It is clear from Fig. 3a that, there is a perfect overlap between the C-Dots absorption/emission spectra with the surface plasmon absorption band for plasmonic nanostructures (i.e., gold and silver), which is required for efficient surface plasmon resonance. However, there is a partial overlap between the absorption spectra of the C-Dots and Ag NPs, while C-Dots show a broad emission spectrum, which provides a complete overlap with the gold surface plasmon absorption spectrum, and consequently prove the metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) concept. Furthermore, upon conjugation of C-Dots with plasmonic nanostructures (i.e. Au and Ag NPs), the absorption intensity of C-Dots is enhanced, Fig. 3b.

As can be seen from Fig. 4(a and b) and Table 1, in the presence of a dielectric polymeric spacer such as Polyethyleneimine (PEI), a remarkable enhancement in both fluorescence intensity and quantum yield of the Plasmonic/C-Dots nanohybrids was observed compared to the close proximal counterparts. This indicates a probable strengthening of the localized fields associated with the surface plasmon (SP) of the metallic part, that leads to the strong coupling effect between excitons and surface plasmons, due the overlap of the local electromagnetic field of excitons in the emissive layer and surface plasmons, leads to significant radiative emission through effective energy transfer in C-Dots [46,48,55]. On the other hand, a quenching effect in the fluorescence of C-Dots could be obtained as a result of the surface plasmon dumping through either resonant energy transfer (RET) between the C-Dots and the metal parts or electron transfer from the C-Dots to the metal in close proximal [46,56].

Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence emission intensity of Plasmonic/Carbon dots nanohybrids at different volumetric ratios (v/v %) of dielectric polymeric spacer (i.e. PEI), upon excitation at 366 nm. For the lower ratio of 3.2 v/v% of PEI, the corresponding the thicknesses of dielectric spacer was about 6.32 ± 2.5 (The Case of Au NPs) and 3.35 nm (The Case of Ag NPs). In such case, the fluorescence intensity of N-Doped C-Dots has a dramatically increase up to 2-folds, owing to the metal-enhancement fluorescence. Although higher thickness of PEI layer about 11.27 ± 3.5 nm (The Case of Au NPs) and 8.79 ± 2.1 nm (The Case of Ag NPs) (See Fig. 1c, d and Fig. S1 , respectively) was obtained, only that the fluorescence emission intensity for N-Doped C-Dots was dropped dramatically. This quenching could be attributed to the dielectric layer of PEI is thick that reduce the effect plasmonic effect on the C-Dots fluorescence.

The PLE properties of C-Dots/Plasmonic have been investigated using different contents of plasmonic nanostructures such as gold and silver nanoparticles on the PLE properties of C-Dots either in the presence or absence of specific polymeric spacer (i.e. PEI), as shown in
Figs. 6 and 7, respectively. Fig. 6 shows the PLE properties of C-Dots/Plasmonic as a function of Au NPs concentration either in presence or absence of Polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mn~1200) as an intra-layer polymeric spacer. The PLE characteristics of hybrid nanocomposite were recorded upon excitation at 366 nm, Fig. 6(a) and (d), or laser irradiation with wavelength at 405 nm (Fig. 6c and f). It is clear that the PLE intensity was proportionally increased as the concentration of Au NPs increased from 46.9 to 57.9 µM, followed by a gradual decrease in PLE properties as a concentration increased from 75.8 to 492.5 µM, as shown in Table (S1). However, PLE properties either in C-Dots/Au and C-Dots/PEI/Au nanohybrids is still demonstrated a remarkable enhancement higher than in C-Dots itself.

The relative quenching efficiency (rQE%) was calculated using the following relationship, as shown in Fig. (S2), and Tables (S1) [57]:
(3)
r Q E % =
(
F
0
− F
F
0
)
× 100
Where, F
0 and F are the integrated PLE intensity of C-Dots in absence, and the presence of plasmonic nanostructures, respectively. It is apparent that a nonlinear profile of rQE% against the concentration of Au NPs occurs (Fig. S2). In addition, the negative (–ve) sign proves that any increase in the Au NPs concentration leads to a remarkable increase in PLE intensity of C-Dots. Also, C-Dots/Au hybrid nanocomposites show remarkable PLE quantum yield enhancement either in the presence or absence of polymeric spacer with a range 1.6–3.3-Fold compared to that of pure C-Dots. Furthermore, the experimental data were in fitness to a modified Stren–Volmer equation (Eq. (4)), rather than the linear form (Eq. (5)) to analyze the fluorescence mechanism in C-Dots/Au hybrid nanocomposites (See Fig. S2 and Table S1), as in the following:[57–61]
(4)
F
0
/ F = 1 +
(
K
D
+
K
S
)
*
[ Q ]
+
(
K
D
K
S
)
[ Q ]
2
(5)
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
S
*
[ Q ]
Where, K
D and K
S are dynamic and static quenching constant, respectively. Fig. S3 shows the plot F
0
/F vs. [Q, Au NPs Concentration] as a function of gold nanoparticles concentration. In the absence of the polymeric spacer, the modified Stren–Volmer equation exhibits a non-linear trend over a wide range of Au NPs concentration from 0→76.8 and 89.6→492.5 µM, respectively. This discrepancy with a linear Stren–Volmer fitting at over total concentrations of Au NPs may be due to the observed broad luminescence, and both of static and dynamic quenching mechanisms occures simultaneously (Fig. S3). In such case, the value of (K
D +K
S ) was ~ − 0.03 M−1, and the –ve sign indicates increasing in PLE intensity of C-Dots. In contrast, with the existence of polymeric spacer, the experimental data exhibit both non-linear behavior over the range from 0→61.8 µM, with the value of (K
D +K
S ) equal to ~ − 0.0246 M−1. The other trend was a linear behavior over a range from 75.8 →492.5 µM due to the static quenching mechanism, where K
S ~ 2.16 × 10−4M−1.

On the contrary to the case of Au NPs, the PLE properties such as the emission intensity of C-Dots/Ag hybrid nanocomposites was proportionally decreased as the concentration of Ag NPs increased from 79→512 µM, Fig. 7a and c. While in the presence of intra-layer spacer of PEI, C-Dots/PEI/Ag hybrid nanocomposites demonstrated a remarkable enhancement in their PLE properties such as emission intensity as a function of the Ag NPs content increased from 0→512 µM, (Fig. 7d and f).

Also, the relative quenching efficiency (rQE%) shows a nonlinear profile with exponential decay behavior of rQE against the concentration of Ag NPs (Fig. S4 and Table S2). In addition, the –ve sign in the presence of PEI proves that any increase in the Ag NPs concentration leads to a remarkable increase in PLE intensity of C-Dots. Furthermore, a 3 – 4.2 fold enhancement in the fluorescence quantum yield (FL-QY) was obtained for C-Dots/PEI/Ag hybrid nanocomposites compared to that of each of pure C-Dots (Fig. 7b). In contrast, C-Dots/Ag nanohybrid show a dramatic decrease in the FL-QY with increasing in the content of plasmonic silver NPs, as shown in Fig. 7e.

Meanwhile, the plot of F
0
/F vs. [Ag NPs Concentration, Q] for C-Dots/Ag nanohybrids, using modified Stern–Volmer equation, shows non-linear trend over a concentration range 0→79 µM of Ag NPs, with the value of (K
D +K
S ), was ~ − 0.061 M−1 indicating the existence of combination between static and dynamic mechanism, followed by a linear fit over the remaining range of Ag NPs, which attributed to static quenching mechanism (Fig. S5a). The Stren-volmer constant (K
S ) was ~ 0.00337 M−1. In addition, the –ve sign indicated to the enhancement of the photoluminescence properties of C-Dots/Ag nanohybrids.

Likewise, the plot F
0
/F vs. [Ag NPs Concentration, Q] for C-Dots/PEI/Ag shows non-linear trend over a concentration range 0→93 µM of Ag NPs, with the value of (K
D +K
S ) was ~ − 0.02118 M−1, indicating the existence of combination between static and dynamic mechanism, followed by a linear fit over the remaining range of Ag NPs which attributed to static quenching mechanism (Fig. S5b). The Stren-Volmer constant (K
S ) was~ − 1.37 × 10−4 M−1. In addition, the –ve sign indicated to the enhancement in both mechanisms of the photoluminescence properties of C-Dots/PEI/Ag nanohybrids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The proposed mechanism to describe the influence of plasmonic on the PLE properties of C-Dots
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the possible interactions between plasmonic material and fluorophores could be summarized as follows: i) the excitation field can be enhanced through a coupling between the Surface Plasmon – assisted generated local field into the incident field. In such case, plasmonic nanostructure works as optical concentrator for the incident source, leading to optical absorption enhancement, ii) plasmonic nanostructures could be used as an excitation source to excite the flurophore, as long as their SP energy is much higher than the band-gap emission of flurophore [35,62,63]. iii) Conversely to pervious pathway, PLE could be enhanced via an efficient energy transfer between the fluorophore and the plasmonic nanostructures, when the exciton energy is greater than SP energy, which attributed to exciton-SP quadrapole interaction [35,64,65]. Depending on the band structures of fluorophore (i.e. HOMO & LUMO) and plasmonic particles (i.e. Fermi level & Wave-function), the PLE quenching or enhancement could be achieved [35,53,54,62,63,66–68]. The enhancement of the PLE properties is due to enhancing the incident excitation field, and increasing the exciton radiative recombination rate [64,69]. Therefore, more excitons can be generated because of the enhanced incident field. Accordingly, remarkable enhancement of the emission intensity observed due to increase the radiative decay rate caused by exciton recombination mechanisms [62,64,69,70].

To gain further insight about the interaction between plasmonic nanostructure and C-Dots, we recorded the picosecond decays of C-Dot, their plasmonic hybrid nanostructures and in presence of PEI polymeric spacer. Fig. 8 displays representative emission decays and the results of multi-exponential fits to the fluorescence decays are summarized in
Table 2(a) and (b). It is readily seen that, for C-Dot/Au nanohybrids, at which the metallic part is in direct contact with the carbon dots, the exciton radiative lifetime is slightly less than that of the pure carbon dots. However, for C-Dot/Ag hybrid nanostructure, the exciton recombination rate decreases slightly.

Therefore, in the presence of PEI as a dielectric polymeric spacer, the plasmonic nanostructure decreases the radiative lifetime of the intrinsic C-Dot exciton recombination process. In addition, the localized electric field on the surface of the plasmonic part increases the rate of charge carrier recombination within the carbon dots [35]. The results confirm the above enhancement and quenching data obtained using Stern–Volmer relation.

To confirm the above mechanism, the band – gap of C-Dots has been determined using cyclic voltammetry measurements of C-Dots in an aqueous solution as shown in Fig. (S6). The onset of oxidation potential of the C-Dots is determined as 1.54 V vs. Normal Hydrogen Electrode (NHE). The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) was calculated according to Bredas et al. [71] and Wang et al. [22,72,73] as following:
(5)
H O M O = − e
(
E
O x d , O n s e t
+ 4.741
)
(
e V
)
(6)
L U M O
=
H O M O +
E
O p t i c a l . B a n d . G a p

Thus, the HOMO of the C-Dots was calculated to be − 6.25 eV, and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) was determined to be − 3.3 eV, the onset optical band gap is 2.95 eV. Whereas, the Fermi level of C-Dots was about 4.75 eV [74], and the Fermi level for both of Au NPs, and silver was 4 and 3.7 eV, respectively [64,75]. Accordingly, the following energy levels diagram has been sketch to show all possible interactions between C-Dots and plasmonic materials either gold or silver in presence or in absence of dielectric spacer (See Fig. 9).

The quenching mechanism has been illustrated as the following; according to the band structures of C-Dots and both Ag and Au NPs shown in Fig. 9a and c, the initial Fermi level of the C-Dots is lower than that of wave function for both of Ag and Au NPs. When the plasmonic nanostructures are in direct contact with the C-Dots, electrons will transfer between the plasmonic nanostructures and C-Dots, and then produce a band bending. The surface plasmon electrons can be excited by the incident field to an excited state, which is higher than the LUMO level in C-Dots. Despite this fact, the excited electrons in the LUMO level can be easily transferred back to the Fermi level of plasmonic nanostructures, because LUMO level in C-Dots is higher than the Fermi level in Ag and Au NPs. Therefore, the band gap emission will be quenched; Fig 9a and c.

The energy of surface plasmon was obtained at absorption band of 520 nm (2.38 eV), and 410 nm (3.02 eV) for gold and silver nanoparticles, respectively. In such case, the SP energy will be close to the band gap emission of C-Dots at around 488 nm (2.54 eV). The energy matching could provide a resonant coupling between the SP electrons and excitons in C-Dots. In another pathway, the incident excitation field could be enhanced due to local field enhancement created by SP electrons in Ag or Au NPs. This will increase the optical absorptivity, resulted in the enhancement of the band gap emission of the carbon dots, Figs. 9(b) and (d).

The results of the present study show the influence of the plasmonic nanostructures, such as gold and silver nanoparticles, on the photoluminescence properties of C-Dots on the absence and presence of PEI as a polymeric dielectric spacer. There is a significant enhancement in the emission properties of the C-Dots/plasmonic nanohybrids, upon the presence of PEI as a dielectric polymeric spacer. In addition, the plasmonic induced enhancement of the optical absorptivity and fluorescence quantum yield (FL-QY) of the C-dots, due to localized field enhancement associated with the SPR in the metal part. Moreover, the plasmonic-exciton coupling for C-Dots/Ag nanohybrids is more pronounced than in C-Dots/Au nanohybrids. This can be attributed to the lower intrinsic losses characterized plasmonic silver than in the plasmonic gold, resulting in narrow SPR with high oscillator strengths and large optical field enhancements. The fluorescence enhancement factor was about 1.76-fold and 2.26-fold of pure C-Dots, for C-Dots/PEI/Au and C-Dots/PEI/Ag nanohybrids, respectively. Also, the FL-QY in C-Dots/PEI/Ag (81%) is much higher than in C-Dots/PEI/Au (65%) nanohybrids. The picosecond decay measurements confirm the presence of plasmonic enhances the rate of the radiative decay of carbon dots. Based on Stren-Volmer calculations, any phenomenon that results in a change of fluorescence intensity (quenching or enhancement), wavelength, anisotropy, or lifetime could be related to the change in the concentration plasmonic nanostructures. Hence, our proposed C-Dots/Plasmonic nanohybrids can be considered as a new candidate of fluorescence sensors that are based on the change in the fluorescence intensity in the presence of the analyte.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Authors are gratefully appreciate Science and Technology Development Fund (STDF) for providing the financial support through Grant ID. 6066, entitled “Novel Hybrid Carbon based Nanocomposites for Biosensors and Biomedical Imaging Applications”, and Grant ID: 1719, “Plasmonic-Semiconductor hybrid Nano-structural Solar Cells: toward Low Cost Great Enhancement of Solar Cell Efficiency”.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.03.045.
